# Testing AirGravQC - FTG Data

Mark Dransfield


```python
from datetime import datetime
print("Now: {:%d %b %Y}".format(datetime.now()))
```

    Now: 16 Oct 2023


> This notebook uses the Vinton Dome survey data to test the AirGravQC Python module and serves as a demonstration of the use of `AirGravQC` on FTG data.

> Throughout this notebook, text indented like this paragraph, and the paragraphs before and after, indicates commentary intended to explain the purpose, or highlight some feature of, the code and text of the nearby cells. The commentary is an addition to the notebook and is intended to provide extra information to someone planning to use `AirGravQC`.

> In order to demonstrate how `AirGravQC` finds and reports errors, this notebook deliberately focusses on errors present in the data. Where there were no errors, the QC specifications have been set to unreasonable values. Consequently this notebook does not accurately reflect the quality of the supplied data. The Vinton Dome data were in fact very good.

> The commentary does not document the software but the usual `CMD-I` click on any function will provide the doc-string help.

> The introduction usually contains a summary of the data and results of QC. It should be written according to the needs of the project but might look something like ...

## Introduction

This notebook summarises the QC of the Vinton Dome field data provided by Bell Geospace in September 2023. The Full Tensor Gradiometer (FTG) survey was acquired in 2002 over an 11 x 11 km area, with 100 m in-line spacing (orientation NS), and covers the Vinton Salt Dome, onshore Louisiana, USA.

We do not have a project number for this survey.

A total of 1,439.4 km of data were provided.

**All data passed review and were accepted with no re-flights.**

The first section of this notebook imports the data into the QC system and summarises the contents. The QC checks of the data are organised into following sections: Reports and Data Formats, Navigation and Positioning, Gravity, and Grids. Otherwise, the notebook is organised in line with the Technical Specifications ("Deed").

> The idea is that every QC check required by the contract and performed by the QC person will be in this notebook. Many checks are performed by running code from `AirGravQC` and the call to the code, and the output, are contained herein. Some checks (for example, that a Daily Report was delivered each day) are not amenable to a check by software but they still are noted, with the outcome of the check, within the notebook. There are also a few additional checks I like to perform. Some fall naturally under the umbrella of "best practice" and some are based on my experience. The reader should not feel obliged to use these checks themselves but they might find them useful.

> I also colour code the results to make it easy to find the areas that require more work.

#### QC Colour Coding

Summary cells are coloured to quickly indicated status against QC. The colours are:

<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
Delivery passed QC.
<div>

<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
Delivery was not checked, or the check is not applicable, or there is a minor shortcoming but is the data are acceptable.
<div>

<div class="alert alert-block alert-warning">
Delivery failed QC and problem has been reported for rectification.
<div>

<div class="alert alert-block alert-danger">
Delivery failed QC and problem might not be readily rectified. This is very rare.
<div>

## Import and Summarise

#### Import required modules, and set filenames for data and plan.


```python
import sys
import importlib as im

import numpy as np
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%matplotlib widget

from pathlib import Path
```

> Set the paths for the software and import. (One day this will require fewer steps.)


```python
local_docs = "/Users/markdransfield/"
src_path = local_docs + "Documents/GitHub/AirGravQC/AirGravQC/"
sys.path.insert(0, src_path)

import pointfiles as gw
import qualityAnalysis as qc
import whizzPlot as wp
import gridfiles as grd
```

> Set the paths for the data. This Vinton Dome delivery did not have a drape plan, nor any gridded data.

> Modify to suit your data.


```python
data_root = "/Users/markdransfield/Documents/GitHub/AirGravQC/examples/"
dx = Path(data_root + r'FTG/VintonDome.xyz')
dh = dx.with_suffix('.hdf5')

# If we had plan data we'd refer to it here, something like ...
# plan_root = data_root
# px = Path(plan_root + r'AGG/Canobie/902212_1.xyz')
# ph = px.with_suffix('.hdf5')
```

### Measured Data

#### Convert measured XYZ data to geoWhizz, and set metadata

> The next step is essential. We need the data in HDF5 geoWhizz format because all the QC functions expect that format. (Be aware that the `xyzToHDF` function overwrites the existing geoWhizz file if it exists.)

> Geosoft XYZ files generally have only the channel names, and no other metadata. We need the attributes of the data at project level, at line number level, and at data channel level.

> Project level metadata attributes can be added here. The example could include others as well as `projectName` ...


```python
gw.xyzToHDF(Path(dx), projectName='Vinton Dome')
```

    Accessing XYZ data in /Users/markdransfield/Documents/GitHub/AirGravQC/examples/FTG/VintonDome.xyz.
    First few records are:
       / ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    
       / XYZ EXPORT [09/21/2023]
    
       / DATABASE   [Z:\Documents\GitHub\AirGravQC\examples\SourceData\VintonDome_BellGeo_FTG_acquisitionQC.gdb]
    
       / ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    
       /
    
       /           X            Y            Lon            Lat     Altitude        Drape      Terrain   Cross1_raw   Cross2_raw   Cross3_raw  Inline1_raw  Inline2_raw  Inline3_raw   TC_Txx_100   TC_Txz_100   TC_Tyx_100   TC_Tyy_100   TC_Tyz_100   TC_Tzz_100      Txx_slv      Txz_slv      Tyx_slv      Tyy_slv      Tyz_slv      Tzz_slv     HHMMSS               Time     YYMMDD 
    
       /============ ============ ============== ============== ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ============ ========== ================== ========== 
    
       /
    
       //Flight 0
    
       //Date 2008/07/06
    
       Line  11
    
    
      Found 10 header records
      Found 91 lines
      Found 28 fields
    
    Creating:  /Users/markdransfield/Documents/GitHub/AirGravQC/examples/FTG/VintonDome.hdf5





    PosixPath('/Users/markdransfield/Documents/GitHub/AirGravQC/examples/FTG/VintonDome.hdf5')



> ... but this way, we demonstrate the use of `updateProject()` to add additional project attributes.

> It also shows the use of `updateCoordFrame()` which keeps track of the coordinate definition and the names of the channels that contain position and time. Many QC routines use position and time, so it saves effort later to have this information in the geoWhizz group attributes.

> Field data are commonly delivered to WGS84, projected to UTM, because that is what GNSS delivers. Transformation to the desired datum happens at the end of the project for the final data.


```python
block_name = 'Vinton Dome Field Data'
gw.updateProject(dh, acquirer='Bell Geospace', blockID=block_name)
gw.updateCoordFrame(dh, lat='Lat', lon='Lon', x='X', y='Y', time='Time', alt='Altitude')
gw.updateCoordFrame(dh, geoDatum='WGS84', htDatum='WGS84', projection='UTM', utmz='15') # just a guess that it is WGS84 / UTM but not important for test data.
```

    Setting BlockID = Vinton Dome Field Data for VintonDome.hdf5.
    Setting Acquirer = Bell Geospace for VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed CoordFrame attribute(s) for VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed CoordFrame attribute(s) for VintonDome.hdf5.


> The line attributes represent a challenge. Line numbers are usually coded to contain a number of impoprtant attributes: whether the line is a traverse, control, repeat or test line; which segment of a number of segments needed to complete the line, and whether it is a re-flight; and what is the line number of the corresponding planned line number. Each supplier uses a different coding system, and sometimes they will use different codings for different projects (or, worst of all (but vary rare), between staff processing the data on the same project!).

> AirGravQC knows only 3 of these systems so far and this will need expanding. So beware!


```python
# gw.updateLineAttributes(dh, line_type='Xcal_can')
```

> The acquirers of the data supplied a separate text file `Channel Descriptions.txt` with the requisite channel attributes metadata which then had to be entered manually via this series of commands to store it in the geoWhizz file created by `xyzToHDF()`. There is some drudgery involved here which you will have to do for your project.

> I did not enter in the supplied descriptions for the channel attributes so all that is happening here is storing the units for each channel. Units are used in some QC checking.


```python
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Altitude', units='m', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Cross1_raw', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Cross2_raw', units='degrees', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Cross3_raw', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Inline1_raw', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Inline2_raw', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Inline3_raw', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Terrain', units='metre', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'X', units='metre', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Txx_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Txz_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Tyx_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Tyy_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Tyz_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'TC_Tzz_100', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Txx_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Txz_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Tyx_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Tyy_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Tyz_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Tzz_slv', units='eotvos', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Drape', units='metre', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Lat', units='degree', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Lon', units='degree', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Y', units='metre', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'Time', units='seconds', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'HHMMSS', description='')
gw.updateChannelAttributes(dh, 'YYMMDD', description='')
```

    Changed channel attribute(s) for Altitude in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Cross1_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Cross2_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Cross3_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Inline1_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Inline2_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Inline3_raw in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Terrain in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for X in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Txx_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Txz_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Tyx_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Tyy_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Tyz_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for TC_Tzz_100 in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Txx_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Txz_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Tyx_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Tyy_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Tyz_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Tzz_slv in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Drape in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Lat in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Lon in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Y in VintonDome.hdf5.
    Changed channel attribute(s) for Time in VintonDome.hdf5.


#### Summary report of measured data

> Summary report of contents of data file. The `reportWhizz()` code can list attributes of a line via the `line=...` keyword, or a channel via the `channel=...` keyword.


```python
gw.reportWhizz(dh)
```

    Whizz Version 1.0
        Acquirer: Bell Geospace
        BlockID: Vinton Dome Field Data
        ProjectName: Vinton Dome
    
    Coordinates
        AltitudeChannel: Altitude
        GeoDatum: WGS84
        HeightDatum: WGS84
        LatitudeChannel: Lat
        LongitudeChannel: Lon
        Projection: UTM
        TimeChannel: Time
        UTMZone: 15
        XChannel: X
        YChannel: Y
    91 lines: total distance flown [km] = 1,439.4
    
    91 lines:
     ['10.000', '100.000', '11.0', '110.000', '111.000', '120.000', '130.000', '131.000', '131.100', '140.000', '150.000', '151.000', '151.100', '151.200', '160.000', '170.000', '171.000', '191.000', '20.000', '211.000', '211.100', '231.000', '251.000', '271.000', '271.100', '291.000', '291.100', '30.000', '31.000', '31.100', '311.000', '331.000', '351.000', '371.000', '371.100', '391.000', '40.000', '401.000', '411.000', '411.100', '421.000', '431.000', '431.100', '441.000', '451.000', '461.000', '461.100', '471.000', '481.000', '491.000', '50.000', '501.000', '501.100', '51.000', '51.100', '511.000', '521.000', '531.000', '541.000', '541.100', '541.200', '551.000', '561.000', '581.000', '60.000', '601.000', '601.100', '621.000', '641.000', '641.100', '661.000', '661.100', '681.000', '70.000', '701.000', '71.000', '721.000', '741.000', '741.100', '761.000', '761.100', '781.000', '80.000', '801.000', '821.000', '841.000', '841.100', '861.000', '881.000', '90.000', '91.000']
    
    28 channels:
     ['Altitude', 'Cross1_raw', 'Cross2_raw', 'Cross3_raw', 'Drape', 'HHMMSS', 'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw', 'Lat', 'Lon', 'TC_Txx_100', 'TC_Txz_100', 'TC_Tyx_100', 'TC_Tyy_100', 'TC_Tyz_100', 'TC_Tzz_100', 'Terrain', 'Time', 'Txx_slv', 'Txz_slv', 'Tyx_slv', 'Tyy_slv', 'Tyz_slv', 'Tzz_slv', 'X', 'Y', 'YYMMDD']



```python
# The Vinton Dome database does not contain flight numbers.
# BUG - should check for 'FLIGHT' attribute and report clean result instead of crashing.
# gw.reportFlights(dh, detailed=True)
```

> Sample times should be constant and in this example they are. Sample distances vary with aircraft ground speed.


```python
gw.reportSampling(dh)
```

    Whizz Version 1.0
        Acquirer: Bell Geospace
        BlockID: Vinton Dome Field Data
        ProjectName: Vinton Dome
    
    Sample time and distance statistics
      Min   = 1.000 s, 40.7 m
      Max   = 1.000 s, 65.4 m
      Mean  = 1.000 s, 51.8 m
      Stdev = 0 s, 3 m


### Plan Data

> Plan data means a located database of planned survey lines with regularly spaced x, y, z positions along the lines. The actual flown positions can be compared with the plan data to see if the aircraft positions meet the appropriate navigation specifications.

> No plan data is available for the Vinton Dome test data. The notebook sections covering the preparation of plan data and checking against relevant QC specifications are therefore skipped in this notebook. They can be seen in other test notebooks.

#### Map showing flown lines

> This provides a visual check that the lines are in about the right location and shows the amount of the survey flown so far.

> Usually this would show the planned (in red) and flown lines (in blue). Not having any plan data, I have just used the same data for both (the red lines are over-written by the blue ones).


```python
wp.linesMap([dh], whizzPlanFile=dh)
```



<div style="display: inline-block;">
    <div class="jupyter-widgets widget-label" style="text-align: center;">
        Figure
    </div>
    <img src='data:image/png;base64,' width=800.0/>
</div>



## Reports and formats
<div class="alert alert-block alert-warning">
OVERDUE REPORTS
<div>

> All of the checks in this section are performed independently of the AirGravQC software and this notebook. But it is convenient to keep all the QC reporting in this one document.

> The acquirer is generally expected to deliver certain reports and data throughout the survey. The data are exxpected in certain formats. This section provides for reporting whether or not these requirements were met.

> Currently this example does not contain any reports. If it did, we would do the following checks.

#### Point located data format
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
ACCEPT
<div>

*Delivered point located field data shall be in ASEG-GDF2 format.*

Actually delivered in Geosoft XYZ. For field data this is not a problem so, even though this does not meet contractual requirements, it is acceptable. Channel descriptions file matches actual supplied channels as required.

#### Gridded data format
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA   
<div>

*The Deed specifies the gridded field data to be supplied.* For Vinton Dome, none were supplied.

> The Deed specifies ERMapper format.

No grids required and none supplied. Not applicable.

#### Static data to be supplied
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*Gravity and navigation processed static readings must be reported to the Customer within 24 hours of every completed flight.*

> No quiescent data were supplied for Vinton Dome. If they had been, they would have been checked and the outcome reported here. Perhaps the report would have been:

Quiescent data has been supplied as required. This has been QC'd on a daily basis and no problems, concerns or failings were observed. All data has passed.

#### Field data to be supplied
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*The Deed specifies the required channels for field delivery.*

> We do not have an actual contract or set of specifications for Vinton Dome. If we had, we might be able to conclude:

Line data - all required channels delivered.

Channel description text file supplied.

#### Delivered reports
<div class="alert alert-block alert-warning">
OVERDUE
<div>

*The Deed requires a Commencement Report before acquisition commences, daily and weekly report through the project, and a final report at the end.*

> As for the previous sections, we do not have any deliverables against this requirement. The following is just an example of what might have been.

Commencement report was received in timely manner and was checked against contract. All requirements met.

Daily reports have been received and have provided the information required under the contract.

Weekly reports - none received, supplier has been asked to rectify.

Final report - only due at end of project.

#### Coordinates and Units
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
ACCEPT
<div>

*As per contract: Correct Coordinate Frame; correct Position Datum, Projected Coordinates and Height Datum; correct units.*

> As we have no contract specifications, we again make up some possible outcome for illustration:

Checked manually.

Coord Frame - PASS

Position Datum - WGS84 incorrect but acceptable for field data.

Projected Coordinate - UTM 15 incorrect but acceptable for field data.

Height Datum - EGM96 geoid incorrect but acceptable for field data.

Units - all correct except gravity which is in mGal instead of µm/s/s. Accept since field data.

> I often accept WGS84/UTM datum/projection and mGal units for field data even though the Deed specifies GDA2020 / MGA and um/s/s. Up to you what you do.

## Navigation and positioning
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
ACCEPT
<div>

#### GNSS
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*The Deed requires a minimum of 5 GNSS satellites visible for AGG acquisition, and PDOP, HDOP and VDOP to not exceed 4.*

> There are no GNSS check data in the Vinton Dome data set (see some of the other test data for examples containing these data). Let's pretend there was and the data passed.

All ok.


```python
# im.reload(qc)
# qc.qc_nav.checkGNSS(dh, 'NumSats', 'PDOP', 'VDOP', 'HDOP', nsats_min=5, max_pdop=4, 
#                   max_hdop=4, max_vdop=4)
```

> The above exemplifies my preferred reporting structure, with the contractual specification first, then any commentary or summary of QC, and finally, the actual QC check performed with its output. (`checkGNSS()` is the first instance in this notebook with all elements of the structure present.)

#### Horizontal Position
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA
<div>

*The Deed requires the horizontal position to not depart from the planned position by more than `x` m for more than `y` m.*

Not applicable. No plan data available.


```python
# qc.checkXYPlan(ph, dh, allowance=40.0, maxCounter=0, maxDistance=1000.0, plot_flag=False)
```

#### Vertical Position
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA
<div>

*The Deed requires the vertical position to not depart from the planned position by more than `x` m for more than `y` m.*

Not applicable. No plan data available.


```python
# qc.checkVertPlan(ph, dh, allowance=20.0, maxCounter=0, maxDistance=1000.0, plot_flag=False)
```

#### Cross-over Heights
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA
<div>

*The Deed requires that the difference in height between traverse and control lines is less than some agreed maximum value.*

There are no control lines at Vinton Dome and no specification so NA.

#### Segment Lengths
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*The Deed requires a minimum line segment length.*

> Let's set a pretend specification of 10 km here.

OK


```python
qc.checkLineLengths(dh, min_len=10.)
```

    Number failed lines = 0


#### Line Overlaps
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA
<div>

*The minimum overlap between segments of a line should be `x` m.*

> This check relies on plan data so is NA for Vinton Dome.


```python
# qc.checkOverlaps(dh, min_overlap=0.6, plot_flag=False)
```

#### Ground Clearance
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*This is a non-contractual check that the aircraft did not approach the ground too closely.*

>  The settings here check if the aircraft came closer than 50 m from the ground. You can change this to a number of your choice, or not bother about this check if you don't want to.

All ok.


```python
qc.checkSafeClearance(dh, 50.0, altitude_chan='Altitude', terrain_chan='Terrain', plot_flag=True)
```

    Number of failed lines = 1.
    
    Clearance too low at 44 m on line 150.00




<div style="display: inline-block;">
    <div class="jupyter-widgets widget-label" style="text-align: center;">
        Figure
    </div>
    <img src='data:image/png;base64,' width=640.0/>
</div>



#### Ground Speed
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
ACCEPT
<div>

*The contract requires the ground speed to not exceed the range +/-`x`% of `y` m/s for more than `z` m.*

> A common specification might be not to exceed 15% around 55 m/s for a distance of more than 1000.0 m. Flying too slow (as has happened a little here) actually provides better spatial resolution in the data so might readily be accepted.

OK.


```python
qc.checkSpeeds(dh, nominalSpeed=55., allowance=0.15, maxDistance=1000.0, plot_flag=False)
```

    Velocities not known - will calculate from positions
    Nominal ground speed 55.0 m/s; allowed 46.8 : 63.2 for < 1000.0 m.
    
     For at least one reading in L171.00, the ground speed was < 42.0 (might be unsafe).
     Checked 91 lines and 199 had some short exceedance(s).
     3 lines failed for exceedance > allowed.
     Total number of full exceedances = 3.
    
    0 exceedances known in the database.
    
    
    L 150.00 too slow for 50.0 sec (2205 m), peak exceedance = 43 m/s.
      From (437604 E 3330053 N) to (439809 E 3330077 N).
    L 171.00 too slow for 39.0 sec (1661 m), peak exceedance = 41 m/s.
      From (438713 E 3334374 N) to (438691 E 3332713 N).
    L 661.00 too slow for 39.0 sec (1736 m), peak exceedance = 42 m/s.
      From (444851 E 3342154 N) to (444841 E 3340418 N).


## Gravity
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

#### Quiescent Noise
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*Deed requires quiescent data collected before each flight and the results delivered in the the daily report. Average signal power density over both complements is required to be below $xE^{2}/rad/sec$.*

> We don't have quiescent data for Vinton Dome and we don't have a specification. Let's pretend ...

Quiescent data were supplied daily by email as required and were checked on arrival. All quiescent data was below 30 E^2/r/s as required.

#### FTG Noise
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*Deed requires that any line with an average in-line sum noise above `y` E be re-flown.*

All data pass.

> The noise depends on turbulence so it is instructive to plot the in-line sum noise against turbulence. This also shows any outliers from the general trend (the plot shows a wide variation but few genuine outliers.)

> A typical value for `y` might be 17.0 E. Here we are going to tighten it down to 10.0 E to force an error!


```python
qc.ilsNoiseVturb(dh, 'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw', noiseSpec=10.0,  vertdispl='Altitude')
```

    461.000: in-line sum = 10.6 exceeds specification of 10.0.




<div style="display: inline-block;">
    <div class="jupyter-widgets widget-label" style="text-align: center;">
        Figure
    </div>
    <img src='data:image/png;base64,' width=640.0/>
</div>



> There are mechanisms (A. Sunderland, Y. Naveh, L. Ju, D. G. Blair, B. Anderson, and M. Dransfield. Acoustic and vibration isolator for a gravity gradiometer. Review of Scientific Instruments, 93(6), 2022.) by which gravity gradiometer noise at a frequency higher than the data frequency band can be down-converted to the data frequency band, resulting in errors in the data.

> The `checkRawFTG` function checks for periods of high amplitude, high frequency signal in the raw gradiometer channels. This is not a check mandated by the Deed, but it is useful.

> The function checks the standard deviation, in a standard rolling window, of each high-pass filtered gradiometer channel and plots diagnostic figures if the standard deviation exceeds some given noise limit.

> My experience is that, for an FTG, a standard deviation above about 50 E is significant and a re-flight should be considered for such data.

> The Vinton Dome data have very much lower high-frequency noise and the noise limit has had to be reduced significantly to 14 E in order that we can see the output of the `checkRawFTG` function. The "rolling stdev" plot is the diagnostic one - the high frequency noise is higher when this plot is high.


```python
qc.checkRawFTG(dh, noiseLimit=14, 
               gradients=['Cross1_raw', 'Cross2_raw', 'Cross3_raw', 'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw'], 
               vertdispl='Altitude')
```



<div style="display: inline-block;">
    <div class="jupyter-widgets widget-label" style="text-align: center;">
        Figure
    </div>
    <img src='data:image/png;base64,' width=640.0/>
</div>





<div style="display: inline-block;">
    <div class="jupyter-widgets widget-label" style="text-align: center;">
        Figure
    </div>
    <img src='data:image/png;base64,' width=640.0/>
</div>



## Statistics
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*None of the following checks are contractually required but they can give some insights to data quality.*

All channel statistics look as expected. OK.

> Checking statistics is a quick way to get an overview over all (or almost all) channels in the database. The aim here is to look for unusual or unexpected behaviour. This might be shown by outliers from the general statistics, values that are clearly different from the expected values, or unusual patterns of some kind.

> `AirGravQC` provides a capability to check that fields whose value should vary smoothly (or not at all) do so. Examples are sample times (as below), or line numbers, flight numbers, or other fields that do not vary at all along a single flight line.

> The checked fields should each vary uniformly with constant slope. For Vinton Dome, only the `Time` channel should have this property.


```python
qc.checkConstantSlope(dh, fields=['Time'])
```

    All fields tested were either constant or of constant slope for all lines tested.


> The second capability simply plots a statistical analysis of each line against line number. On the vertical axis, filled-in squares show the mean value of the channel for the line, vertical terminated lines show +/- one standard deviation, and open circles show the minimum and maximum.

> A lot of information is condensed here which is very useful. You can run your eye quickly over the plots looking for outliers, and quickly check the vertical scales to ensure that the values are in about the right range.


```python
qc.allChanStats(dh, ['Altitude', 'Cross1_raw', 'Cross2_raw', 'Cross3_raw', 'Drape', 
           'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw', 'Lat', 'Lon', 
           'TC_Txx_100', 'TC_Txz_100', 'TC_Tyx_100', 'TC_Tyy_100', 'TC_Tyz_100', 'TC_Tzz_100', 
           'Terrain', 'Time', 
           'Txx_slv', 'Txz_slv', 'Tyx_slv', 'Tyy_slv', 'Tyz_slv', 'Tzz_slv', 'X', 'Y'])
```

</div>

</div>



## Grids

#### Delivered Grids
<div class="alert alert-block alert-info">
NA
<div>

*For Vinton Dome, there was no requirement for grids of field data to be delivered and none were.*

> The Deed does not have specifications on the quality of gridded field data but grids are a quick and easy way to spot any artefacts in the data. So they are useful in checking for "best practice".

> The function `checkErsHeaders()` is useful when there are supplied grids.

> In any case, I always generate grids from the located data (see below).


```python
# qc.checkErsHeaders(ersp)
```

#### Generated Grids
<div class="alert alert-block alert-success">
PASS
<div>

*There are no Deed specifications on the grids except "best practice".*

All the grids look okay and there are no serious artefacts.

> I grid and image almost every channel in the database (here I have omitted only `DATE`). Channels that are directionally dependent have the mean for each line removed ("mr") before gridding. For some channels it is more useful to examine the grid of the first difference along each line ("d1").

> Some images here look a little peculiar and deserve comment.

> - Altitude: The variations from line to line are obvious but the range of altitudes is quite reasonable.
> - Cross1_raw and all the raw gradients: Gridding the data after correction for the mean along each flight line is useful because the raw gradients are direction dependent. The ctighter line spacing in the central area shows more variation. This is all as expected for raw data.
> - Lat and Lon: Gridding the first difference of these channels shows speed variations. Since the traverse lines are north-south, and the control lines east-west, these variations are dominant in the other direction.
> - TC_T##: The original terrain corrections show some striping. The stripes coincide wth stripes in the Altitude grid so are to be expected.



```python
z_chans = ['Altitude', 'Cross1_raw', 'Cross2_raw', 'Cross3_raw', 'Drape', 
           'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw', 'Lat', 'Lon', 
           'TC_Txx_100', 'TC_Txz_100', 'TC_Tyx_100', 'TC_Tyy_100', 'TC_Tyz_100', 'TC_Tzz_100', 
           'Terrain', 'Time', 
           'Txx_slv', 'Txz_slv', 'Tyx_slv', 'Tyy_slv', 'Tyz_slv', 'Tzz_slv', 'X', 'Y']
mr_chans = ['Cross1_raw', 'Cross2_raw', 'Cross3_raw', 'Inline1_raw', 'Inline2_raw', 'Inline3_raw']
d1_chans = ['X', 'Y', 'Lat', 'Lon', 'Time']
grd.grid_n_image(dh, z_chans, mr_chans, d1_chans, 100.0)
```

    Gridding and imaging Altitude
    91 lines; total number of fids in whizz file = 27865.
    Processing (x, y, z) = (X, Y, Altitude). Altitude in m.
    Data points cover region: [ 436298.  448078. 3327615. 3344419.]
    z range (1st to 99th percentile) (69.81, 102.8)
